title: Dog Dragging Hind Legs: Causes, Emergency Signs & Triage Guide
meta_desc: If your dog is dragging their back legs, it is a veterinary emergency. Learn about hind limb paralysis, urgency levels, and how to get help fast.
content_html:
TL;DR: Dragging hind legs is a high-priority veterinary emergency typically caused by serious neurological or spinal issues; immediate medical intervention is critical for the best chance of recovery.

What is hind limb paresis or paralysis in dogs?

When a dog drags their hind legs, it is medically referred to as hind limb paresis (partial loss of movement) or paralysis (total loss of movement). This condition typically suggests a serious neurological or musculoskeletal issue, where the signals from the brain are not reaching the legs correctly. It can manifest as 'knuckling' (walking on the tops of the paws), scuffing the feet, or a complete inability to stand or move the rear end.

Is my dog dragging their back legs a medical emergency?

  • Dragging the hind legs is a high-priority emergency, as sudden loss of mobility often points to Intervertebral Disc Disease (IVDD), spinal cord trauma, or a vascular event like a 'spinal stroke.'
  • These conditions are time-sensitive; the sooner a dog receives veterinary care, the higher the chance of regaining mobility.
  • If your dog is also showing signs of intense pain, loss of bladder control, or loss of 'deep pain' sensation, seek an emergency veterinarian immediately.

How can a photo or video help the vet triage my dog?

  • A short video of your dog attempting to move allows the veterinarian to visually assess the severity of the neurological deficit—such as whether the dog is 'ataxic' (wobbly) or completely paralyzed—before you arrive.
  • A photo showing if your dog's paws are 'knuckling' (tucked under) helps the medical team prepare the necessary diagnostic tools for your arrival.
  • Providing visual evidence helps the triage team prioritize your dog's care based on the observed severity of their mobility loss.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Peripheral nerve injuries, particularly those affecting the sciatic nerve or its branches, are common causes of hind limb dragging in dogs. Damage can occur secondary to pelvic fractures, retrograde placement of intramedullary pins in the femur, or injections of irritating substances near the nerve. With nerve root avulsion, the dog may bear little to no weight on the affected limb, dragging the dorsal surface of the paw. Avulsion of caudal nerve roots results in loss of sensation on the caudal surface of the limb, with variable loss on the cranial surface. In severe cases involving complete avulsion, amputation may be necessary due to self-mutilation or injury from dragging. Sensory ganglioneuritis, though less common, can also cause ataxia and decreased proprioception in all limbs, sometimes accompanied by self-mutilation; diagnosis requires electrodiagnostic testing and nerve biopsy.

Chapter: Neurology

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1244)

faqs.question: What is the most common cause of a dog suddenly dragging its back legs?
faqs.answer: Intervertebral Disc Disease (IVDD) is one of the most common causes, particularly in breeds with long backs like Dachshunds or French Bulldogs, where a spinal disc herniates and compresses the spinal cord.

faqs.question: Can I give my dog aspirin or ibuprofen at home for this?
faqs.answer: No. Never give human pain medication to a dog. Some human NSAIDs are toxic to dogs, and others can interfere with the life-saving steroids or medications the vet may need to administer.

faqs.question: Is it possible for a dog to recover from hind leg paralysis?
faqs.answer: Recovery is possible depending on the cause and how quickly treatment is initiated. Many dogs regain mobility through emergency surgery, intensive crate rest, and physical therapy.
